content.problemStatement:
There is a floor with dimensions K×NK \times NK×N, where KKK is 1, 2, or 3. You have an infinite supply of tiles of two types:


Type 1: 2×22 \times 22×2 square tiles

Type 2: 3×13 \times 13×1 tiles.
The 3×13 \times 13×1 tiles can be rotated and be used as 1×31 \times 31×3 tiles as well.

You need to use these tiles to cover the entire floor, in such a way that every cell of the floor is covered by exactly one tile and all the tiles lie completely within the floor. You have to find the number of different ways to doing so. Since the answer might be large, output it modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T. Each test case contains two integers, K and N, separated by a space.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing a single integer, which is the number of different tilings possible, modulo 10^9 + 7.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
There is no way to tile a 1×51 \times 51×5 floor with the given tiles. Hence the answer is 0.


There is only one way to tile a 1×61 \times 61×6 floor.




There are two ways to tile a 2×62 \times 62×6 floor.





There are eight ways to tile a 3×63 \times 63×6 floor.

Explanation:
There are 13 ways to tile a 3×77 \times 77×7 floor.

Explanation:
There are 3 ways to tile a 2×72 \times 72×7 floor.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we will use dynamic programming and recursion. We will first initialize a 2D array dp with dimensions (K+1) x (N+1) and fill it with zeros. Then, we will iterate over each possible value of K and N, and for each pair (K, N), we will calculate the number of ways to tile a K x N floor using the given tiles. We will use memoization to store the results of subproblems and avoid redundant calculations.
